facts.title: Seollal, Lunar New Year
facts.fact: Seollal, the Lunar New Year, is one of Korea's biggest holidays. Families gather, hold ancestral rites, and children bow to elders to receive money and blessings.
facts.korean_en: I meet my family on Lunar New Year.
facts.note: On 설날 people eat **떡국** (rice-cake soup); eating it is playfully said to add a year to your age.

facts.title: Chuseok, the harvest festival
facts.fact: Chuseok, the autumn harvest festival, is often called "Korean Thanksgiving." People travel to their hometowns and share songpyeon, half-moon rice cakes.
facts.note: **명절** is a traditional holiday, as opposed to 휴일, an ordinary day off.

facts.title: Buddha's Birthday and lotus lanterns
facts.fact: Buddha's Birthday (**부처님 오신 날**) falls on the eighth day of the fourth lunar month. Temples and streets are decorated with colorful lotus lanterns, and a lantern parade lights up Seoul.
facts.korean_en: On Buddha's Birthday I look at the lotus lanterns.
facts.note: The lantern festival is called **연등회**; it is on UNESCO's list of intangible cultural heritage.

facts.title: Children's Day
facts.fact: Children's Day (**어린이날**) is celebrated on May 5 and is a public holiday. Parents take their kids to parks, zoos, and amusement parks and give them gifts.
facts.note: The word **어린이** (child) was popularized by writer 방정환, who helped create the holiday.

facts.title: Hangeul Day
facts.fact: Hangeul Day (**한글날**), on October 9, celebrates the creation of the Korean alphabet by King Sejong in 1446. It honors Hangeul as a uniquely scientific writing system.
facts.note: King 세종대왕 published Hangeul in a text called **훈민정음** ("the proper sounds to instruct the people").

facts.title: Liberation Day
facts.fact: Liberation Day (**광복절**), on August 15, marks Korea's freedom from Japanese colonial rule in 1945. The name means "the day the light returned."
facts.note: On 광복절 many people hang the **태극기** (national flag) outside their homes.

facts.title: Memorial Day
facts.fact: Memorial Day (**현충일**), on June 6, honors soldiers and citizens who died for the country. At 10 a.m. a siren sounds nationwide and people observe a moment of silence.
facts.korean_en: On Memorial Day we observe a silent tribute for the country.
facts.note: On 현충일 the national flag is flown at half-mast, called **조기**.

facts.title: Jeongwol Daeboreum, first full moon
facts.fact: Jeongwol Daeboreum (**정월대보름**) is the first full moon of the lunar year. People eat nuts to ward off illness and wish for good luck while gazing at the bright moon.
facts.korean_en: On the first full moon day, I look at the full moon.
facts.note: Cracking nuts with your teeth on this day is called **부럼 깨기**, believed to keep your skin healthy all year.

facts.title: Dano, early summer festival
facts.fact: Dano (**단오**), on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, welcomes early summer. Traditionally women washed their hair in iris water and people enjoyed swinging and ssireum wrestling.
facts.korean_en: On Dano I ride the swing.
facts.note: The **강릉단오제** (Gangneung Dano Festival) is a famous celebration recognized by UNESCO.

facts.title: Pepero Day
facts.fact: Pepero Day (**빼빼로 데이**), on November 11, is an unofficial day when friends and couples exchange thin stick-shaped chocolate biscuits. The date 11/11 looks like four Pepero sticks.
facts.korean_en: On November 11th, I give Pepero as a gift.
facts.note: It is a commercial holiday, not a traditional one; the same date is also **가래떡 데이**, promoting rice cakes.

facts.title: Solar and lunar calendar dates
facts.fact: Some Korean holidays follow the solar calendar (**양력**) and others the lunar calendar (**음력**). Seollal and Chuseok move each year because they are set by the lunar calendar.
facts.korean_en: Lunar New Year is set by the lunar calendar.
facts.note: Many Korean calendars print the **음력** date in small numbers under the **양력** date.

facts.title: Charye, ancestral rites
facts.fact: Charye (**차례**) is a memorial rite held on Seollal and Chuseok to honor ancestors. The family sets a table of food, bows together, and shares the meal afterward.
facts.korean_en: On traditional holidays we hold ancestral rites.
facts.note: The verb for performing a rite is **지내다**, as in 차례를 지내다 or 제사를 지내다.

facts.title: First sunrise of the New Year
facts.fact: On January 1, many Koreans travel to the east coast or a mountain to watch the first sunrise (**해돋이**). They make a wish for the new year as the sun appears.
facts.korean_en: On New Year's Day I go to see the sunrise.
facts.note: **정동진** on the east coast is one of the most popular spots to watch the first 해돋이.

facts.title: Baegil, the 100th-day celebration
facts.fact: Baegil (**백일**) celebrates a baby's 100th day of life. In the past, surviving the first hundred days was a milestone, so families share rice cakes to wish the child long life.
facts.korean_en: On a baby's 100th day, we share rice cakes.
facts.note: It was once traditional to share **백일 떡** with 100 people to pray for the baby's long life.

facts.title: Dol, the first birthday
facts.fact: Dol (**돌**) is a big party for a baby's first birthday. At the highlight, called doljabi, the baby picks an object from a table, which is said to predict the child's future.
facts.note: If the baby grabs the thread (**실**) at **돌잡이**, it is said to mean a long life.

facts.title: Sebae, the New Year bow
facts.fact: Sebae (**세배**) is the formal New Year's bow children give to their elders on Seollal. In return, elders offer words of wisdom and gift money called sebaetdon.
facts.korean_en: On Lunar New Year, we bow to our elders.
facts.note: Children often say **새해 복 많이 받으세요** ("receive many blessings in the new year") while bowing.

facts.title: Substitute holidays
facts.fact: Korea uses a substitute holiday system (**대체공휴일**): if a public holiday falls on a weekend or overlaps another holiday, the next weekday becomes a day off instead. This guarantees people still get the rest day.
facts.korean_en: If a public holiday is on Sunday, we rest the next day.
facts.note: The official term is **대체공휴일**, literally a "substitute public holiday."
